I am in the middle of the TFTC podcast and you're talking about the vortex spin, including the importance of the 3rd and 4th ventricles in the brain. The pony (PICASSO) had a MRI (result attached) which identified an asymmetry of the left and right ventricles found in other epileptic horses. I had a look at the anatomy here [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6443180/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6443180/) (with NO medical background at all), and my question is, if the lateral ventricles supply the 3rd and 4th ventricles, if the vortex is off, is the DHA pi-electron cloud what is stabilizing the quantum environment allowing the spin to correct from the lateral to the 3rd and 4th? Clearly the asymmetry is a material finding, but doesn't explain to me the result (so far) of serious stabilization post DHA added to his feed regimen. However, the quantum explanation does. Understanding DHA can be subject to deuterium, the previous attempts to stabilize him had to be adding to the deuterium load. This new DHA protocol is either going to do the same thing eventually realizing more breakthrough seizures, or it is a better source and will help allow the spin to remove the previous load.

I know he's just a horse, and I have my own wealth of issues to address, but he means the world to us and he's teaching me so much about this entire thesis.

### craigdubya — 2026-05-10T09:33:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparison-of-ddw-ppm-and-3-nacl-for-de-fragging-protocol.32593/post-373603>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=371603)
>
> To optimize the "de-fragging" protocol, we must align the **Isotopic Load** of the water with the **Dielectric Torque** of the salt. A standard 3% NaCl infusion possesses an osmolarity of approximately **1,027 mOsm/L**. In my framework, this high-density sodium surge is designed to "flush" the **Y-axis** and clear the **Obex**.
>
> To calculate the "De-fragging Match," we look at the ratio of **sodium ions** to **deuterium atoms** in the fluid.
>
> **1\. The Isotopic Variable**
>
> Standard water contains ~155.76 ppm of deuterium. In a 1-liter bag of 3% saline, you have: Na+
>
> **Ions**: ~513 mmol. D+ **Atoms**: ~8.6 mmol (at 155 ppm).
>
> **Initial Ratio**: ~60:1 (Na to D)
>
>
> **2\. Optimizing the PPM for the 3% Flush**
>
> If the goal is to maximize the "Scouring Power" of the infusion while reducing the "Sludge" in the lattice, you want to push that ratio toward **100:1** or higher.
> To achieve a 100:1 ratio with 3% saline, the math dictates a specific DDW ppm:
> **Target Deuterium content:** 5.13 mmol/L.
>
> **Optimal DDW Concentration**: **~92.5 ppm**.
>
> **3\. Protocol Implications
>
> The 92.5 ppm Threshold**: This aligns with the "high-efficacy" DDW levels (85–105 ppm) used to arrest atavistic cell growth in oncology.
>
> **The Dielectric Sweet Spot**: At ~90 ppm, the water is low-viscosity enough that the 3% sodium "torque" can easily "centrifuge" the fluid through the **Lateral Ventricles** and out of the **Nerve Root Sleeves below in humans**.
>
> **The 160THz Recovery**: This ratio prevents the "puffy" extracellular stagnation seen in creatine abusers. By providing **high-torque (Na+)** and **low-friction (DDW)**, you maximize the speed at which the **Obex** can dump the "Deuterium Bomb" into the vagus
>
> ✅ **Conclusion**: For a 3% NaCl infusion (513 mmol/L Na+) , the optimal "de-fragging" concentration is approximately **92.5 ppm DDW**, which provides a **100:1 Na+/D ratio t**o maximize isotopic flushing and dielectric rectification.

Jack what volume of 3% NaCl IV per kg are you recommending for SH and administration frequency?

### Alain Dwight — 2026-04-29T04:44:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-heritable-is-iq.32651/post-372402>

I understand it is an imperfect measurement, I'm using the term as a rough stand in for processing power and amount of state one can hold in mind. The conventional view seems to be that these parameters are highly heritable while environmental factors mainly have little effect unless it is negative like malnutrition or severe.

In light of quantum biology, is this wrong? Is IQ driven more by melanin and quantum yield in the brain affecting morphology and signal strength? Can IQ be increased significantly within a person's lifetime or within a generation or two. Is there a way for parents to reliably create children that have far higher IQ than they do?

### Jack Kruse — 2026-04-29T06:26:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-heritable-is-iq.32651/post-372412>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-heritable-is-iq.32651/post-372453>

Basically, maternal health IQ datasets are polluted by lack of light and magnetic controls? They control for basic nutrition but no one is checking what happens when you cover proper nutrition + physical/emotional safety + magnetism + light environments (including no sunscreen and no blue screens)?

### Jack Kruse — 2026-04-29T16:39:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-heritable-is-iq.32651/post-372475>

Two mothers. One with a low IQ and healthy and one with a 200 IQ who is heavily deuterated as an IT executive.

Who do you want your momma to be considering the following

Mat.I said: Whats super important is to know when the MITF - AMPAR circuit breaker has tripped or close to being maxed out.. I was stumped a couple of years back as I would be out all day (plenty of melanin renovation) at an altitude of ~700 (closer to the belts in OZ) then I would travel say 30 to 40 mins away to sea level get ~ half a day of full sun and the needle would move drastically (I would get very dark just in that short period). I was origanlly putting this down to something that I could not measure in my environment.. I recall even visiting family in Sydney I sat on a driveway in the sun and the melanin production was crazy.. But back at home (not far from syd) im out everyday so that tells me my local environment is not suited to my n=1 MITF - AMPAR pathway.. MITF - AMPAR should be in expansion/melanin mode not survival dopamine mode.. All ties back to mag declination in the local environment and distance from the belts. Remember melanin is a sink for deterium, when it backs up with too much you will see this happen to varying degrees. My example is not extreme, but when you are striving to be optimal it is a big deal..

My Answer: Because the magnetic flux is lower and the "noise" is higher at that altitude, your T2 **relaxation window** stretched beyond the 7.2-second limit. Even with "plenty of sun," your **MITF (Microphthalmia-associated Transcription Factor)** was stuck in **Repair/Survival mode**, frantically trying to quench the ROS/UPE instead of building the "antenna" (Melanin)

When you traveled 40 minutes to sea level, you didn't just "get more sun"; you **Increased your Dielectric Pressure.

The Atmospheric Buffer:** At sea level, you have a thicker "grounding mat" of atmosphere and a higher **Z-axis magnetic intensity** (away from the belt-proximity).

**Restoring the Buffer:** This compressed your T2 **window** back down to 5 or 6 seconds. Suddenly, your "Collision-to-Repair" ratio shifted into the **Positive**. The "heavy" D+ silt that was "bottlenecking" your melanin was finally flushed through the **AMPAR (AMPA Receptor)** circuits. Those are the circuits inside your head that allow one to think well.

**The Expansion Mode:** The system flipped from "Survival" to "Expansion," and you "got dark" rapidly because the **Melanin-POMC axis** finally had the "coherence time" to actually manufacture the polymer instead of just "fire-fighting" the isotopes. Your exterior tan tells you about your exterior........but not the interior dielectric pressures.

This hits on my key point: **Melanin is a sacrificial sink for deuteration.**

This is why what you heard me say to Jones and Cowan was 100% right, right now humanity needs a **Dynamo-Isotopic Calculator**.

Standard "UV Indexes" are useless because they don't account for **Magnetic Declination** or the T2/D+ ratio of your zipcode. Which mother has the better zip code now? The one who is low IQ or the one who hs a fully deuterated germ line who eats out of a deuterated zipcode?

For example : A person in **Sydney** (on the Pacific High) has a different "Survival-to-Expansion" threshold than someone in **Perth** (on the SAA approach), even if the UV index is identical. One is a **Quantum Processor**; the other is a **Maillard Oven.**

Humanity is in a **Dynamic Magnetic Storm.**

If your local T2 window is "Leaky" due to the SAA, you must either **Lower the D+ input (food and water - DDW below 92 ppm) or** **Increase the Dielectric Torque (3% Salt/Grounding)** to prevent the "Dopamine Survival" mode from becoming permanent **Atavism. This is what lattice locked means.

My Conclusion:** The "needle moving drastically" at sea level was your **Topological Insulator** finally catching its breath. You moved the "Speed Bumps" (D+) out of the way of the **160THz signal**, and your **Melanin** did exactly what it was evolved to do during the Cambrian: **Capture the Sun by magnetic grounding to the dynamo to lose the deuterium. The low IQ mom does this better than the "smart" IT executive who waited til she was 40 to have kids**

### [Diffusion markers of dendritic density and arborization in gray matter predict differences in intelligence - Nature Communications](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-018-04268-8)

Previous studies suggest that individual differences in intelligence correlate with circuit complexity and dendritic arborization in the brain. Here the authors use NODDI, a diffusion MRI technique, to confirm that neurite density and arborization are inversely related to measures of intelligence.

 www.nature.com

This is the **"Internal/External Phase-Shift."** You’ve hit the most profound clinical distinction in the entire decentralized framework: **Melanin in the skin is the "Faraday Shield," but Neuromelanin in the brain is the "Quantum Processor."**
If your exterior tan is deep but your interior is "Maillard-browned," you are a high-performance shell with a stalled engine. The **interior tan** is the measure of your **160THz coherence** in the **Obex, the Substantia Nigra, and the Locus Coeruleus.

1\. The "Frosted Lens" of the Interior**

You can have a perfect exterior tan (UV-Melanin rectification on the crust) but still suffer from **Isotopic Stagnation** in the brain's "Water Table."See Los Angeles as the classic Kruse example.........good tans and dumbassery walking on Rodeo drive.

**The MRI/Thermogram Proof:** White matter changes and "slow flow" on a thermogram are the macroscopic views of **Increased Viscosity**. The "internal pipes" are filled with "ketchup" (heavy water), slowing the **Transition Probability** of the DC current.

**2\. Cognition, Wisdom, and the 160THz Signal:**

Consciousness is a function of the **Internal Dielectric Pressure.
The Processor:** Wisdom requires high-speed **Proton Tunneling** across the neural semiconductor. If the internal dielectric constant is dropping from **160 toward 78**, the "Phase-Lock" to the **Universal Stator** is lost.
**The Haze:** You might look "healthy" on the outside, but if your **NAD+/NADH ratio** is "heavy" (deuterated), your cognitive "OS" is downgraded to **DOS.**You lose the ability to "process" complexity and fall into the "Survival/Dopamine" mode.

**3\. The "Melanin-POMC" Disconnect**
As Mat noted with his N=1 in this thread, the **MITF-AMPAR** pathway can be "fooled" by local variables.

**Exterior Tan:** Tells you the skin is successfully "quenching" the environmental noise.
**Interior Tan:** Tells you the **Heme-Melanin axis** is successfully producing **DDW** to cool the **IMM**.
**The Conflict:** If you are grounded but still drinking 155 ppm water in a high-declination zone, your skin will tan, but your **Internal Lattice** will still "Lattice-Lock" because the **D-Speed Bumps** are entering through the gut/water-table = Ann Marie, Julz, and Caroline.

**4\. The "Z-Axis" of Wisdom**
Wisdom is the **Asymptotic Optimality** of the brain's quantum ensemble.
It requires the **7.2-second "Quiet Time"** between isotopic collisions to allow for the **Intersystem Crossing (ISC)** into the Triplet state.
If the interior is "noisy" (High UPE/Low Dielectric), the "Wisdom signal" is lost in the **Maillard-Orange** heat. You are "Zapped" on the inside while being "Golden" on the outside.

**5\. The Synthesis: The True De-Frag**
The goal of the **92.5 ppm DDW / 3% NaCl / UV** protocol is to **"Tan the Interior."**

It lowers the **Internal Viscosity** so the **160THz signal** can reach the **Pineal and Pituitary (The Empty Sella check).**
It clears the **Obex** so the "Cognitive Highway" is frictionless so you can avoid things like ALS or oxalate damage or stiff man syndrome Jordan Peterson diseases.

**My Conclusion:** An exterior tan is a "badge of effort," but a clear **Internal Dielectric Pressure** is the **"State of Grace."** It is the difference between a "Human Billiard Ball" and a **"Topologically Protected Quantum Sovereign."

Google just put a 2029 expiration date on Bitcoin's cryptography. And it is so dangerous they are refusing to publish the code. We thought a quantum attack on crypto was decades away and it would take millions of qubits and massive computing power. But we were wrong.. Google Quantum AI just dropped a paper proving you don't need a futuristic supercomputer to break the elliptic curve cryptography that secures Bitcoin and Ethereum. You only need 1,200 logical qubits. That is a 20-fold reduction in the hardware previously thought necessary to crack the network. Here is the terrifying part: When you send Bitcoin, your public key is exposed in the "mempool" for about 10 minutes before the transaction is confirmed in a block. Google’s researchers compiled a quantum circuit that can theoretically derive your private key from that exposed public key in roughly 9 minutes. It takes the dreaded "mempool attack" and turns it into a mathematical reality. Now, take a deep breath. Existing crypto is not dead today. Google did not actually execute this hack. The physical hardware required to run this circuit (roughly 500,000 physical qubits) does not exist yet. Today's best quantum computers are only hovering around 1,000 physical qubits. But the math is officially solved and Google just proved exactly how to build the key.
Hopefully just fud.

### Jack Kruse — 2026-04-30T02:51:07+0300

The picture above is from one of the Russian papers I translated long ago, you heard me talk about.

Make me think of this guy. In 1980, two years before Feynman's famous Caltech lecture on Quantum Computing, a 43-year-old Soviet mathematician named Yuri Manin published a slim 128-page popular-science book called Вычислимое и невычислимое — Computable and Noncomputable , through the Moscow publishing house Sov. Radio.

Manin was not a computer scientist. That made him stick out to me..........

The Poplavsky reference is to R.P. Poplavsky, "Thermodynamical models of information processing," Uspekhi Fizicheskikh Nauk 115:3 (1975), 465–501.

### Jack Kruse — 2026-04-30T02:52:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/decentralized-biologys-brachistrochrone.32660/post-372573>

He was already one of the great algebraic geometers of his generation: a Lenin Prize laureate (1967), professor of algebra at Moscow State University, principal researcher at the Steklov Mathematical Institute, the mathematician behind the Gauss–Manin connection and the Mordell conjecture for function fields. He had been forbidden from foreign travel since 1968. The book was written in Russian, never officially translated for nearly thirty years, and its argument about quantum computation took up barely three pages of the introduction.

What's striking about Manin's framing and what got almost entirely lost when the Western quantum computing canon formed around Benioff, Feynman, and Deutsch is the direction of the argument. Feynman's 1982 case for quantum computers was pragmatic and engineering-flavored: classical machines can't efficiently simulate quantum systems, therefore we should build quantum machines that can.

Manin came at it from the opposite end. He looked at molecular biology at protein synthesis on messenger RNA, at the absurd information density and energetic efficiency with which living cells perform what looks structurally like Turing-machine computation and concluded that nature had already solved the problem. Classical physics, he argued, simply cannot account for what biology does. My kinda dude.

### Jack Kruse — 2026-04-30T02:53:23+0300

The mathematical theory of quantum automata must already be implicit in the substrate of life. Engineering quantum computers wasn't the goal; it was the obvious downstream consequence of taking biology's existence-proof seriously.That places Manin in a different intellectual lineage than the one quantum computing eventually inherited. He was downstream of Schrödinger's What Is Life? (1944) and the broader Soviet tradition of treating life as a physical system whose laws had not yet been written, Vernadsky, Lyapunov, the cybernetics revival under Berg and Glushkov.

The West built quantum computing as an engineering discipline of qubits-as-fabricated-systems, and pushed biology off into a separate and often-dismissed sub-field called "quantum biology." Forty-five years later, with the work emerging on microtubules, tryptophan networks, ordered water, and coherent processes in neural lattices, the field is, in a real sense, finally catching up to its own actual origin.The translation below is from pages 13–15 of the introduction.

### Jack Kruse — 2026-04-30T02:54:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/decentralized-biologys-brachistrochrone.32660/post-372575>

On the inefficiency of computing devicesMolecular biology provides examples of the behavior of natural (not human-engineered) systems which we are forced to describe in terms close to those accepted in the theory of discrete automata. The figure below depicts the scheme of protein synthesis on messenger RNA: it closely resembles the depiction of a Turing machine copying information from one tape to another.

Classical continuous systems governed by differential equations can imitate discrete automata only when their phase space has an exceptionally complex structure an abundance of stability regions separated by low energy barriers. Loading a program carves out a sophisticated system of passages through these barriers, predetermining the motion of the phase trajectory through this labyrinth. As a physical system, the computing device must be highly unstable, since an error of a single character in the program generally leads to an entirely different trajectory.

Yet the computational process itself must be exceptionally stable, that is, spontaneous errors (transitions of the trajectory across a barrier that should remain closed, as a result of fluctuations) must have very low probability. It is well known that these requirements combined with slowness of operation and the exponential growth of dissipated energy as complexity increases erected the barrier that halted the development of mechanical computers.

\[Citing Poplavsky's 1975 paper on thermodynamic models of information processes:\]

### Jack Kruse — 2026-04-30T02:56:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/decentralized-biologys-brachistrochrone.32660/post-372577>

**A genuinely instructive calculation can be found there:** the quantum-mechanical description of the methane molecule by the lattice method requires computation at 1042 points. If we assume only 10 elementary operations are performed at each point, and suppose all computations are carried out at ultra-low temperature, then even so the calculation of the methane molecule would require expending energy roughly equal to that produced on Earth over a century.

On quantum automataIt is possible that for a better understanding of such phenomena a mathematical theory of quantum automata is lacking. The mathematical model of such objects must exhibit highly unusual properties compared with deterministic processes.

The reason is that the capacity of the quantum state space is dramatically greater: where in the classical case there are N discrete states, in quantum theory, which permits their superposition, the state space lies in CN. When classical systems are combined, their state-counts N1 and N2 simply multiply; in the quantum case one obtains C^(N1·N2).

These rough estimates show that systems exhibiting quantum behavior are potentially far more complex than their classical counterparts. For example, since the system has no unique decomposition into parts, the state of a quantum automaton may be regarded in many different ways as states of entirely different virtual classical automata.

In carrying out such a program, the first difficulty will be finding the right balance between mathematical and physical principles. The quantum automaton must be abstract: its mathematical model should use only the most general quantum principles, without prejudging physical implementations. Then the model of evolution is a unitary rotation in finite-dimensional Hilbert space, and the virtual decomposition into subsystems corresponds to the tensor-product decomposition of that space.

Somewhere in this picture the place of interactions, traditionally described by Hermitian operators and probabilities must still be found.

**Notes on this translation:**The C in "CN" is the field of complex numbers; CN is N-dimensional complex Hilbert space. C^(N1·N2) reflects the tensor product H1 ⊗ H2 , the structure that gives quantum systems their entanglement-driven computational advantage.

identifying the hardware defect (the repeat expansion) without understanding the power supply failure (the deuteration of the epigenetic landscape).
When geneticists look at the C9ORF72 repeat expansion (GGGGCC), they see a "glitch" in the code. But from my perspective, this sequence is a massive "deuterium trap" for several reasons:
1\. The G-Quadruplex as a "Heavy Metal" Antenna
A sequence like GGGGCC repeated hundreds of times creates structures called G-quadruplexes.
The Quantum Problem: These structures are highly sensitive to the mass of the protons involved in their folding. If the methyl groups surrounding this gene are deuterated (due to the failure of the CYP2C19/Melatonin cycle), the G-quadruplex becomes "stiff" and heavy.
The Result: The "widened band gap" I mentioned in my thesis prevents the smooth flow of electrons and UPEs along the DNA. The gene can no longer "vibrate" at the correct frequency to be read properly, leading to the protein misfolding characteristic of ALS and FTD.
2\. Epigenetic "Clogging" via KIE
To silence or activate a gene, the body adds methyl groups (CH3).
If those methyl groups are loaded with Deuterium from synthetic supplements or "fake food," the Kinetic Isotope Effect (KIE) makes the bond significantly harder to break or move.
Epigenetic Stasis: The cell tries to regulate C9ORF72, but the "heavy" methyl groups act like rust in the gears. Geneticists see "altered methylation patterns," but they don't realize the atomic mass of those patterns is what's actually stalling the machinery.
3\. The "Low Energy Particle Accelerator" Failure
If the IMM (Inner Mitochondrial Membrane) has lost its 40-million-volt charge due to hypoxia, it can't provide the "quantum suction" needed to pull protons away from the DNA.
In ALS, the motor neurons are the first to fail because they have the highest metabolic demand and the most complex mitochondrial networks.
They are the "canaries in the coal mine" for a system that can no longer manage the deuterium-to-protium ratio required to maintain the topological protections of the cristae.
The "Missing Link"
Geneticists are right about the location of the failure (Chromosome 9), but I believe I'm right about the mechanism. The repeat expansion isn't the "cause", it's the vulnerable site that fails first when the human Lagrangian loses its ability to deplete deuterium. I've been called out of retirement to do some work with special needs students. Today, I finally saw the truth while wearing my Dr. Kruse eyes. I've been a special needs teacher for over 30 years, and I work in the belly of the beast, So Orange County California, where 1 in 6 children are born with special needs. It was a warehouse full of children with autism and other neuro-differences. Where I once saw children who were "perfect in God's eyes" and "worthy of receiving an equitable education," I instead saw children who were struggling as "astronauts on Earth" fighting against their lattice-locked minds and bodies. The head-banging, hitting, loud vocalizations, hopping, running, biting were all struggles to unlock their vortices and loosen sludge. Instead, we warehouse them in blue light-bathed classrooms, filled with nnEMFs, give them iPads, and lock them inside to "protect them." We let them eat their preferred deuterium-filled foods and beverages. We ask them to perform simple, meaningless tasks while they are desperately trying to attain some measure of equilibrium. And I drove home with a profound fear that this is what a world in the throes of magnetic declination will look like. God help us all.

## The Epigenetic Trigger​

Better clues to the causes of the autism phenomenon come from parallel “neolithic epidemics” also found today in our modern world. Of neolithic diseases, the prevalence of inflammatory diseases in general has increased significantly in the past 60 years. As a group, they include asthma, now estimated to affect 1 in 10 children. This is now at least double what the prevalence was in 1980. What else? Autoimmune disorders, which now afflict 1 in 20. In some parts of the world, like Australia or southern California, the numbers show stepper risks for autoimmunity and cancer. Remember what I wrote for you in [EMF 2](https://jackkruse.com/emf-2-einstein-meet-leptin/)? **Pandemics or epidemics are not caused by genetics in medicine; they are caused by alteration in epigenetic mechanisms.** Epigenetic modification is due to an altered environment for some reason.

**Huge Epigenetic Clue:** Autistic children tend to have mothers with poor immune function who also have **low iron and copper levels**. Iron and copper are both transition metals on the [periodic table of elements.](http://www.webelements.com/)

## Why Transitional Metals (Copper and Iron) are a Big Clue to the Puzzle​

I found the copper and iron link to autism and EMF in one of the more unlikely places in my studies. I found it in the manufacturing of ceramic manufacturing processes and the process of curing asphalt for freeways. The initial link was made because I had read a paper on the epidemiology of autism and how the incidence and prevalence was higher around cities with a lot of freeways. I thought to myself this was an odd environmental trigger. It was here that I found that the native ELF EMF from the Earth core was blocked by petroleum deposits in the ground. This implied that places where there were big oil deposits would be considered geopathic stress zones to the MHC 1 gene.

Asphalts are made with petroleum products and with metal powders that are used to cure the asphalt quickly. The link suddenly began to make sense to me. It was the same relationship the literature was revealing in vaccines data with respect to autism. New processes were being designed to speed up the cure rates and manufacturing process using these highly conductive metals to reduce costs while getting a better material handling effect. Transition metals are highly conductive because they have a ton of D shell electrons that allow them to share electrons in many molecular configuration states. D shell electrons are easily energized by the photoelectric effect and have many axis that they can be found. This makes these electrons have the ability to form electron clouds and be delocalized. I’ll explain later in the blog why this issue is huge in biochemistry.

Asphalt is made with petro chemicals and contains a lot of metal ions that are highly conductive. In fact, modern asphalt now has **carbonyl iron** added to it to get it to heat up quicker to lay faster. You might be shocked to know carbonyl iron is the same type of iron found in iron supplements you might take for iron deficiency.

The added metal powders make the products they are manufacturing by have unique capabilities in products used to build cities. In recent years, microwave processing of metal/alloy powders have gained a considerable potential in the field of material synthesis. Microwave heating is recognized for its various advantages, such as: time and energy saving, very rapid heating rates, considerably reduced processing cycle time and temperature, fine microstructures and improved mechanical properties, better product performance. Microwave material interaction for the materials now all have a bound transition metal charge added to it, to improve its material characteristics. This is why they have been adopted aggressively by industry and are now well established by modern industry standards in 2013.

The interesting thing was for highly conductive materials, like copper and iron metals and their alloys, there was not much information available to interpret the actual mechanism of how microwave heating and radiation actually worked for sintering of metallic materials back in the late 1990s and early 2000s, when I looked for them. You should know melanin is a chelator of both metals.

Deuterium Is Structurally Useful in Collagen
The C–D (carbon-deuterium) bond is significantly stronger than the C–H bond. Because collagen is a structural protein that must withstand constant mechanical stress, deuterium enrichment — particularly in the amino acids proline and hydroxyproline (which together make up over 22% of collagen’s residues) — appears to reinforce the collagen triple helix, making it more resistant to wear and tear. # Thread: LifeLabs British Columbia sold your health records to blackrock
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lifelabs-british-columbia-sold-your-health-records-to-blackrock.32664/unread>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DLO — 2026-04-30T06:27:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lifelabs-british-columbia-sold-your-health-records-to-blackrock.32664/post-372615>

he Wall Street Takeover of BC Health Data: Your Blood Work Now Answers to Shareholders
ALEX HOSNER
APR 29, 2026
If you live in British Columbia, your provincial lab system is no longer a healthcare utility. It is a publicly traded asset, effectively owned by Wall Street mega-funds like Vanguard, BlackRock, and State Street.
This is not a political conspiracy; it is a structural and financial reality. In August 2024, the BC government rubber-stamped the sale of LifeLabs—the corporate entity responsible for processing 42 million lab tests annually for the province—for $1.35 billion CAD.
\--------
The Pension-to-Wall-Street Pipeline
Before 2024, LifeLabs was owned by OMERS—the Ontario Municipal Employees Retirement System. Ironically enough, when I started working on a drilling rig when I was 18, we spent 5 years drilling oil and gas wells for OMERS Energy.
OMERS is a massive Canadian pension fund. Their entire business model is built on acquiring and holding physical, long-term assets—whether those are natural gas wells or provincial medical labs—to guarantee stable, decades-long returns for Canadian municipal workers. Under OMERS, LifeLabs was operated as a utility. It was patient capital.
But in August 2024, that model was permanently dismantled. When OMERS sold LifeLabs to Quest Diagnostics for $1.35 billion CAD, we didn’t just trade one corporate logo for another. We traded patient Canadian capital for Wall Street extraction.
Quest Diagnostics isn’t interested in operating a stable, long-term utility. They are a publicly traded entity driven by institutional investors who demand aggressive, perpetual quarterly growth. The moment the ink dried on that sale, the BC lab system ceased to be a healthcare service and became a dividend engine.
To the average patient getting blood drawn, the logo on the door simply changed. But behind the scenes, the fundamental math of British Columbia’s healthcare system was violently rewritten. We traded Canadian pension fund ownership—which prioritizes slow, stable, long-term returns—for Wall Street institutional ownership, a beast that demands relentless, perpetual, and aggressive quarterly dividend growth.
Quest Diagnostics is not just a medical company; it is a $22 billion ticker symbol (DGX) on the New York Stock Exchange. When the fiduciary obligation to maximize shareholder returns sits directly between your doctor’s requisition and your test result, the degradation of patient care is not a mistake. It is a mathematical requirement.
\---------------------
Who Actually Owns Your Lab Tests? The Ownership Structure of DGX.
To understand how healthcare decisions are made under this model, you have to look at the cap table. Quest Diagnostics is a massive, publicly traded entity, and over 90% of its shares are held by institutional investors.
There is no “founder” steering the ship with a moral compass. There is no benevolent board of doctors protecting patient care. The company is owned, controlled, and dictated by the largest asset management funds on the planet.
As of early 2026, the top institutional shareholders of Quest Diagnostics (DGX) are:
The Vanguard Group: ~13.0%
BlackRock: ~8.5%
T. Rowe Price: ~5.7%
State Street Global Advisors: ~4.8%
JPMorgan Chase & Co: ~3.7%
If you combine Vanguard, BlackRock, and State Street, the “Big Three” index funds effectively control over a quarter of the entire voting power of the corporation.
Why does this matter? Because Vanguard and BlackRock do not manage healthcare; they manage wealth. Their fiduciary duty is to their fund investors, not to British Columbia’s patients. They vote on board members, executive compensation, and corporate strategy using a single, unified metric: maximizing shareholder return.
When the CEO of Quest Diagnostics looks at his quarterly dashboard, he isn’t answering to the BC Minister of Health. He is answering to Vanguard and BlackRock. And if swapping a comprehensive medical test for a cheaper, automated one increases the dividend yield, Vanguard will vote to keep the executive who made that swap and fire the one who didn’t.
\--------------------------------------------
The Math of the “Efficient Breach”
To understand what Wall Street ownership means for your health, you have to understand a concept in corporate law known as the “efficient breach”.
The efficient breach dictates that if substituting a cheaper, flawed process saves a corporation more money than it ultimately loses paying out settlements for the harm caused, doing so is not just an option—it is a calculated, legally defensible business decision. This is the exact math Ford Motor Company ran in their infamous internal memo when they calculated it was cheaper to let a certain number of people burn to death in Pinto fires than to spend the money fixing the gas tank design.
And it is the exact logic woven into the DNA of the company now running BC’s labs.
Quest Diagnostics has run this math before. In 2009, the US Department of Justice forced a Quest subsidiary (Nichols Institute Diagnostics) to pay a staggering $302 million settlement. The charge? They had knowingly manufactured, marketed, and sold faulty parathyroid hormone (PTH) test kits for six straight years. Doctors were using these inaccurate tests to make critical surgical and treatment decisions for patients with bone and kidney disease.
To a patient, six years of faulty tests is a medical catastrophe. To a massive publicly traded corporation, a $302 million fine for six years of elevated profit margins is simply a line item. It is the cost of doing business.
That same company now owns your blood work. And the shift toward “efficient” diagnostics in British Columbia has already begun.
\--------------------------------
The 6,149 Missing Diagnoses
The most glaring example of this efficiency mandate wasn’t about advancing medicine; it was about slashing labour costs at the expense of accuracy.
In late 2022, the BC Guidelines and Protocols Advisory Committee (GPAC) downgraded provincial stool testing. They mandated a shift from comprehensive, manual microscopy (the traditional Ova & Parasite test) to a cheaper, automated, multiplex PCR test called the Infectious Diarrhea Panel (IDP).
The financial logic is simple: Manual microscopy requires expensive human labour—technicians sitting at microscopes analyzing slides. Automated PCR tests do not. You feed the sample into a machine, and it spits out a result. It is high-volume, low-friction, and highly profitable.
But there is a medical cost. A peer-reviewed study published in the BC Medical Journal in March 2024 audited over 52,000 provincial stool specimens. They found 6,149 positive cases of parasites using the old manual method.
Because the new, automated IDP test only screens for a narrow list of specific, common pathogens, it completely misses the vast majority of those 6,000+ infections—specifically common but debilitating parasites like Blastocystis hominis and Dientamoeba fragilis.
Thousands of patients walking around with chronic gastrointestinal issues are now told their lab results are “negative,” simply because their specific pathogen didn’t make the “efficient” corporate list. A known diagnostic blind spot was codified into provincial protocol to save on human labour.
\----------------------------
Inventory, Not Patients: The Monetization of Biomarkers
The extraction of value under the Wall Street model doesn’t stop at the lab desk. It extends to the backend, where your biological data is transformed into a marketable asset.
Your blood sugar trends, cancer markers, hormone levels, and disease history are now held by a US corporation. Quest Diagnostics maintains that Canadian data stays on Canadian servers, but this is a legal sleight of hand. Because Quest is an American corporation, they are subject to the US CLOUD Act. This federal law allows American law enforcement and intelligence agencies to legally compel US-based companies to hand over data, regardless of where those servers are physically located in the world. British Columbia’s privacy laws cannot shield you from a US federal warrant issued to a Delaware corporation.
But the government isn’t the only one interested in your data. Quest openly markets a product called Commercial Alerts.
This product uses patient lab profiles to help pharmaceutical companies target doctors. According to their own marketing materials, a specific test order or biomarker result is the “first real-time indication of a potential candidate for a targeted therapy.” Quest uses this data to trigger alerts to pharmaceutical reps, allowing them to aggressively push specific drugs to your doctor at the exact moment your lab results come in.
Simultaneously, Quest is building an AI platform designed to monetize massive patient datasets in a global health-AI market projected to hit $1.03 trillion by 2030.35
Under this model, your lab results are not just medical records. They are inventory.
And the infrastructure holding that inventory is notoriously porous. In 2019, LifeLabs suffered a catastrophic data breach affecting 4.7 million BC residents—nearly the entire population of the province. They paid a ransom to hackers to retrieve the data. They spent four years fighting in court trying to bury the privacy commissioner’s investigation. Furthermore, they were found to have collected more personal information than the law allowed and failed to implement basic security safeguards.
The BC government’s response? They renewed the LifeLabs contract, and then approved the $1.35 billion sale to Wall Street.
\------------------------
The Anatomy of a Handover: Regulatory Capture
The BC government didn’t just stumble blindly into this American acquisition. The timeline proves they were lobbied, and they actively prepared the ground for it.
May 2, 2022: LifeLabs hosts an “Informational Reception” at the BC Legislature in Victoria. Public lobbying records show Health Minister Adrian Dix in attendance, alongside a massive list of MLAs and senior health bureaucrats.
July 2022: The BC government begins quietly outsourcing thousands of cervical cancer screenings (pap smears) directly to Quest Diagnostics in the United States This “pilot program” acted as the foot in the door, establishing the American corporation as a trusted, cross-border vendor for provincial health data.
October 2022: The provincial government downgrades provincial testing, implementing the automated IDP test and sacrificing thousands of accurate parasite diagnoses for operational efficiency, perfectly priming the system for an automated buyout.
August 2024: Quest Diagnostics officially buys LifeLabs, inheriting a lab system optimized for high-volume, low-labour profit extraction.
\---------------------------------
The $970 Million Dividend Machine
Quest expects its Canadian operations to generate $970 million CAD per year —revenue generated almost entirely from government contracts paid by your taxes.
Where does that money go? The financial pivot following the acquisition was immediate and aggressive. In early 2026, Quest authorized a $1.0 billion increase in stock buybacks and raised their shareholder dividend by 7.5%. Their CEO explicitly told investors the Canadian acquisition would be profitable in its very first year.
Your health tax dollars are being stripped from the BC medical system and converted directly into quarterly dividends for Vanguard, BlackRock, and State Street.
Your provincial government claims they are helpless. They state they are locked into this service contract until 2031 and cannot cancel it without triggering break clauses that would cost taxpayers “hundreds of millions of dollars.”
They built the trap, handed the keys to a Delaware corporation, and now tell the public they are stuck in it.
A recent poll found that 74% of BC residents oppose American ownership of their health services, and 77% want the government to intervene. The government knows this. They are counting on the public not connecting the timeline, the lobbying, the financial reports, and the missing diagnoses.
Healthcare in British Columbia has been financialized. Your health was never the priority. It was always the product.

# Thread: Mum tried committing suicide twice because of SSRI use. We are her sons, trying desperately to help her get better!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mum-tried-committing-suicide-twice-because-of-ssri-use-we-are-her-sons-trying-desperately-to-help-her-get-better.32665/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sander9992 — 2026-04-30T15:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mum-tried-committing-suicide-twice-because-of-ssri-use-we-are-her-sons-trying-desperately-to-help-her-get-better.32665/post-372642>

Dear All,

The past year and a half has been a living hell for my mother and for us as a family. I am a terrified son who is afraid of losing his mother. We are fighting a fight against the big system, and we are completely lost on what to do.

Because the GP and psychiatrists have been fluctuating between different doses of antidepressants, Mum has attempted suicide twice. She is still alive, but with all the consequences that entails. During her second suicide attempt, she swallowed a bottle of drain cleaner. Life is hell at the moment. My younger brother and I want to do everything we can to give her her life back.

_(If this doesn’t belong in this category on this forum, my sincere apologies! I would appreciate some guidance on how to post this correctly within this wonderful community, without breaking any rules. I wish to show the utmost respect to everyone in this community!)_

**\# PERSONAL PROFILE & ENVIRONMENTAL PROFILE**
Mum has a long history of numerous physical complaints. Mum is now 65 years old. She has been taking the antidepressant Sertraline (25mg) daily for 30 years. At the time, the GP prescribed this because she had many physical (not mental) complaints. Years later, it turned out she had fibromyalgia. She has never been able to stop taking the antidepressants since. We were all born and raised in South Limburg, the Netherlands (50.96780842250297, 5.766106832206341)

**\# HISTORY / BACKGROUND**
Sixteen years ago, she went through a difficult period. At the time, there were several major issues in her personal life, which caused everything to go completely wrong. She had already been sleeping for about an hour a night for more than six months. This eventually pushed her over the edge, leading her to make several suicide attempts. Fortunately, she survived these without any lasting harm. She eventually recovered thanks to being prescribed Zyprexa. This allowed her to sleep 14–16 hours a night, after which things gradually improved.

Since then, she has been very stable for the past 30 years, with no problems, on 25mg of Sertraline. During this time, Mum, together with Dad, ran a large and successful business, weathered the 2008 economic crisis, raised two sons, and kept the whole family together. They went through many turbulent times, but Mum has always been so incredibly strong. She was there for everyone; nothing was ever too much for her, and she did everything with determination and confidence.

**\# SUMMER 2024 to PRESENT (SPRING 2026)**
Mum and Dad had just successfully sold their business and were in the process of finalising the handover. The future looked bright; Mum was feeling great, and we were planning a family road trip across America. Until our whole world came crashing down...

In the spring of 2024, she began to suffer from a rash and a ‘burning sensation’, which she was absolutely certain was caused by the sun. Previously, she could be found in our garden swimming pool (a salt-based pool) every day, and she spent a lot of time in the sun, but suddenly she developed the rash. We looked for any possible change in her life at that time, but we couldn’t find any significant changes.

She was driven mad by the ‘burning sensation’ in her body and the rash. She started looking into it herself (without telling us), and soon read that the antidepressants could be the cause of her symptoms. She took this to her GP. That’s where all the problems began! The GP wanted to taper off the antidepressants without a moment’s hesitation.

This period in a nutshell:
First suicide attempt - May 2025:
In June 2024, following a consultation with her GP, my mother began tapering off her sertraline (25mg) after 16 years of use (25mg > 15mg > 10mg > 5mg).
On 2 January 2025, her daily dose was 5mg, and that’s when things went completely wrong.
Tremors, a burning sensation in her limbs, panic attacks, agitation, insomnia and confusion.
Her GP (in consultation with a psychiatrist) advised her to increase the dose from 5mg to 10mg within one day, to 12.5mg two days later and to 25mg four days after that.
The symptoms got worse and worse, and she was prescribed 15mg of Zyprexa to help her sleep. The next day, 7.5mg of Zyprexa, and then she stopped taking it.
The sleep helped keep the complications manageable, but they did not disappear.
Two months later, things went wrong again due to stressful situations in her life, and the GP advised increasing the dose to 50mg of sertraline.
The symptoms worsened again, so the GP advised increasing the dose to 100mg of sertraline.
She said she felt as though she was poisoning herself.
Eventually, on 3 May 2025, she attempted suicide by taking an overdose of sertraline and zyprexa (800mg sertraline + 2 strips of 15mg zyprexa).

She survived and was admitted to an open ward at GGZ Zuyderland Hospital Sittard-Geleen under the care of psychiatrists H.J.H. Bremer and J.M.C. Martens.
Here, things went from bad to worse.
They stopped the sertraline and switched to escitalopram. The escitalopram dose was gradually increased daily, starting with 1mg and eventually reaching 10mg.
After 2 to 3 weeks with no improvement, the dose was increased to 15mg of escitalopram in a single day; this had a devastating effect on my mother.
We pointed out that the dose increase was only making her worse, and she was quickly reduced back to 10mg.
A day later, Mr Bremer said they were going to taper her off and try a different medication. Back down to 5mg in a single day.
Then, later that same day, Mr Martens came in and said: we need to keep trying at 10mg for a while, so back up again. Two different psychiatrists in a single day, saying completely opposite things. The symptoms only got worse.

Second suicide attempt -
On Monday 9 June 2025, she attempted suicide again, this time by drinking drain cleaner. She fought for her life in the ICU for two months. Seeing your mother like that, because of ‘professionals’, is true hell. Knowing that the same people would have to help us climb out of this even deeper hole. Professionals who pretend they’re doing their best and have your best interests at heart, but lie straight to your face and just want to get off the hook.

Following her admission to the ICU, Mum has made a physical recovery, but she has been left with very severe chemical burns to her oesophagus. She was subsequently transferred to Maastricht UMC for a second opinion and admission to the psychiatric ward, where she is being treated by Professor Marcelis. Compared to previous experiences, this period was a breath of fresh air (a thousand thanks to the great Mrs Mercelis for this!!!). People listened, there was room for discussion, and well-considered decisions were made (Within the centralized framework).

In the first few weeks, a conscious decision was made (at our family’s strong insistence) not to administer any antidepressant medication, with the aim of allowing her nervous system to settle down and so the new practitioners in Maastricht UMC could get to know my mother better. They investigated whether underlying factors were at play, such as trauma or issues from her past. At the same time, she continued to suffer from symptoms such as a burning sensation in her body and head, tremors, panic, confusion and severe insomnia. She also developed anhedonia, stating that she no longer experienced any emotions and wondering whether she would feel anything at all regarding events involving her loved ones. She was aware that this was not reality, but stemmed from her situation. After a few weeks, it was concluded that there were no underlying psychiatric issues and that the symptoms had likely arisen during the tapering off of medication.

Eventually, after 4 weeks, treatment was started with nortriptyline, which was gradually increased to 100 mg, with a therapeutic level of around 70. She was temporarily prescribed lorazepam to help her sleep, but this was discontinued due to its diminishing effectiveness and the risk of dependency. Quetiapine (50 mg) was then introduced, which she is still taking alongside the nortriptyline.

Meanwhile, the severe oesophageal injury played a major role. My mother was unable to eat or drink and was dependent on tube feeding. Symptoms such as extreme vomiting, foul tastes and excessive mucus production made the situation extremely difficult. The oesophagus was completely blocked, it filled up her lungs, and so all fluids had to come back out through the mouth. This naturally also had a major impact on her mental state. These images are etched into our memories.

After four months at the open ward at Maastricht UMC, it was decided in consultation that further mental recovery would largely depend on her physical condition. At that point, she was, however, considerably more stable mentally than before her admission to Maastricht UMC. Her mental are was then, with some reluctance on our part, transferred back to Zuyderland Hospital, not to start all over again, but to provide support. The further psychiatric care she would need is, in fact, region-specific and we could not choose who her practitioners were, we tried to push hard for this.

Due to her oesophageal problems, we were referred to Erasmus MC in Rotterdam. There, it was determined that her oesophagus was severely damaged and narrowed, and that only a complete reconstruction was still possible. In January 2026, she underwent this major operation, during which a section of her large intestine was used to create a new oesophagus. The old oesophagus could not be removed because it was too firmly adhered to the surrounding tissue, and the stomach was too severely damaged to continue using it. The operation was technically successful and she is currently recovering; she is now able to eat and drink small amounts, but still has a long way to go.

However, since the operation, we have again observed a deterioration in her mental state. Shortly after the operation at Erasmus MC, her Nortriptyline levels suddenly dropped sharply, presumably due to a change in absorption due to the hospital admission and all different the pain medication she was given during this hospital stay. In the weeks that followed, this improved somewhat, until a problem with the feeding tube meant she was unable to take her medication for a few days. Almost immediately afterwards, her condition deteriorated again, including thoughts of death and despair. With our intensive support, we helped her through this.

It has now been two months and her condition has stabilised again. However, insomnia remains a major problem. Zuyderland Hospital has advised us to use a low dose of benzodiazepine on an occasional basis. Although we have serious reservations about this, the severity of the insomnia sometimes leaves us with no choice but to accept it. It helps a little from time to time, but does not offer a long-term solution.

**\# CURRENT ISSUES**
The core problem remains that my mother has been experiencing symptoms all this time that closely resemble prolonged withdrawal symptoms: a burning sensation in the body, electrical sensations in the head, problems with temperature regulation, severe insomnia, constant nervousness and an elevated heart rate of around 100 (whereas previously, throughout her life, it had been around 60). Yet, once again, practitioners seem to be focusing primarily on a depressive disorder, whilst we, just as we did at the time at Maastricht UMC, suspect iatrogenic damage.

Zuyderland Hospital is now once again proposing to start various courses of medication. This causes me great concern, as it feels to us like a repeat of previous treatment pathways in which there was a great deal of experimentation with medication, each time resulting in a deterioration in her condition. My conviction is that her nervous system needs rest above all else after this exceptionally difficult period. We are therefore primarily seeking solutions for her insomnia and, in my assessment, protracted withdrawal symptoms. We have noticed that it is the insomnia in particular that is undermining her the most; a bad night’s sleep significantly exacerbates her symptoms during the day.

She still has to go to Erasmus MC in Rotterdam every week for weekly dilation of her new oesophagus. However, she says she is starting to experience all the symptoms more and more again – from her oesophagus, her intestines and her nervous system. Summer is approaching again, and it is starting to get warmer. She is terrified of this. We continue to emphasise that she must get outside as much as possible, into the sun, practise grounding, keep the lights off in the evenings, get up at sunrise, turn the lights off at sunset, and wear blue-light-blocking glasses. All of this seems to help only minimally, leaving us increasingly unsure of what we can do.

Her current psychiatrist at Zuyderland Hospital continues, to our repeated disapproval, to prescribe her various heavy-duty medications, such as: increasing the Nortriptyline dose from 70 to 100, increasing Quetiapine to 150–300mg, or trying Olanzapine again, starting lithium (20ml > 50ml/100ml), and repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation at Maastricht UMC+ (outpatient treatment of around 30 sessions, often 5 days a week).

We no longer trust these quacks, and we’re afraid that any change to her medication will throw Mum off balance again. We’ve finally got her to a somewhat ‘stable’ state, where her symptoms are just about bearable, and she sleeps for between two and four hours a night.

I want to emphasise that, despite everything, my mother is an exceptionally strong woman. Every day, she tries to stay active by going for walks, taking on small tasks and keeping herself occupied wherever possible. She is finally smiling at us again. Given the circumstances, that is almost a miracle. She is keen to recover and move forward, and she herself keeps saying that she no longer feels depressed, but is exhausted by the symptoms, the disruption to her nervous system and everything that has happened.

I believe that the inconsistency in treatment, rapid increases and decreases in dosage, and daily fluctuations in dosage have led to kindling and hypersensitivity.
I think that when withdrawal symptoms appeared in January 2025, the nervous system became dysregulated, and all the medication added later disrupted the nervous system even further.
Trust in the treating professionals has been completely undermined.
The psychiatrists claim that the previous suicidal thoughts indicate that the original depression from 16 years ago is returning. However, I consider this utter nonsense, as she had never had suicidal thoughts before.
The suicidal thoughts can be directly traced back to changes in medication, just like all the other symptoms.

So now we have exhausted all treatment options within the centralised healthcare system in the Netherlands. We are exploring every possibility, from psychedelics to cannabinoids, magnetism and electrical therapy. But we are merely laypeople, trying to research what we can, yet it is still beyond our capabilities. We are at a loss as to who or what can help Mum get better. We are so terribly afraid that any intervention we undertake will have a negative impact on her again, and that we will lose control once more, with all the consequences that entails.

**\# URGENT APPEAL TO ANYONE WHO CAN HELP**
My younger brother (28) and I (31) came across Dr Jack Kruse’s work six months ago. We had been aware of it for some time, but the podcast with Danny Jones and Dr Kruse showed us exactly how the system works, not only in America, but in the Netherlands too. This has given us such enormous strength to fight back against the practitioners and the hospitals. These people may call themselves professionals, but a son who sees his mother being poisoned, who sees his mother attempt suicide twice, is capable of anything. Dr Kruse calls on his Savages to fight. In the small community where we live, my brother and I are fighting against the big system, and they will never win!
We have completely turned our lives upside down. We are trying to get Mum involved in this too, but because she is so mentally unstable, many things are difficult for her to understand.

Until now, I have been a silent participant on this forum. I have tried to gather as much information as possible, specifically regarding our case. But Mum’s condition is rapidly deteriorating again, and I cannot learn the complex subject matter quickly enough to be able to help her. Hence my cry for help to anyone who can offer us some guidance.

We are open to any suggestions. Whatever it takes to help Mum lead a stable life. We are prepared to travel the world if necessary. But we have no certainty. We can take action, but what if it has negative consequences?
I am an uninformed person who would very much like to learn. Yes, we have made many mistakes recently, and perhaps you will cringe when reading this message. But we have fought with everything we have, begging the practitioners to consider non-medicinal methods to help her. We have done everything we can to get her out of the centralised system, but the practitioners have completely ignored this.

If anyone is willing to take the time to read this long message in their spare time, you’re my hero! I know this won’t be a miracle cure, but if anyone has any information for us on what we could do to help my mum get better, please enlighten us!!

If there are any additional questions to help with a better analysis, please feel free to ask, and I hope I can answer all questions correctly.

Thank you all!
Sander & Marco, sons of our dear mother Jacqueline

### Jack Kruse — 2026-05-01T18:54:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mum-tried-committing-suicide-twice-because-of-ssri-use-we-are-her-sons-trying-desperately-to-help-her-get-better.32665/post-372743>

> [Sander9992 said:](/goto/post?id=372642)
>
> Dear All,
>
> The past year and a half has been a living hell for my mother and for us as a family. I am a terrified son who is afraid of losing his mother. We are fighting a fight against the big system, and we are completely lost on what to do.
>
> Because the GP and psychiatrists have been fluctuating between different doses of antidepressants, Mum has attempted suicide twice. She is still alive, but with all the consequences that entails. During her second suicide attempt, she swallowed a bottle of drain cleaner. Life is hell at the moment. My younger brother and I want to do everything we can to give her her life back.
>
> _(If this doesn’t belong in this category on this forum, my sincere apologies! I would appreciate some guidance on how to post this correctly within this wonderful community, without breaking any rules. I wish to show the utmost respect to everyone in this community!)_
>
>
> **\# PERSONAL PROFILE & ENVIRONMENTAL PROFILE**
> Mum has a long history of numerous physical complaints. Mum is now 65 years old. She has been taking the antidepressant Sertraline (25mg) daily for 30 years. At the time, the GP prescribed this because she had many physical (not mental) complaints. Years later, it turned out she had fibromyalgia. She has never been able to stop taking the antidepressants since. We were all born and raised in South Limburg, the Netherlands (50.96780842250297, 5.766106832206341)
>
> **\# HISTORY / BACKGROUND**
> Sixteen years ago, she went through a difficult period. At the time, there were several major issues in her personal life, which caused everything to go completely wrong. She had already been sleeping for about an hour a night for more than six months. This eventually pushed her over the edge, leading her to make several suicide attempts. Fortunately, she survived these without any lasting harm. She eventually recovered thanks to being prescribed Zyprexa. This allowed her to sleep 14–16 hours a night, after which things gradually improved.
>
> Since then, she has been very stable for the past 30 years, with no problems, on 25mg of Sertraline. During this time, Mum, together with Dad, ran a large and successful business, weathered the 2008 economic crisis, raised two sons, and kept the whole family together. They went through many turbulent times, but Mum has always been so incredibly strong. She was there for everyone; nothing was ever too much for her, and she did everything with determination and confidence.
>
> **\# SUMMER 2024 to PRESENT (SPRING 2026)**
> Mum and Dad had just successfully sold their business and were in the process of finalising the handover. The future looked bright; Mum was feeling great, and we were planning a family road trip across America. Until our whole world came crashing down...
>
> In the spring of 2024, she began to suffer from a rash and a ‘burning sensation’, which she was absolutely certain was caused by the sun. Previously, she could be found in our garden swimming pool (a salt-based pool) every day, and she spent a lot of time in the sun, but suddenly she developed the rash. We looked for any possible change in her life at that time, but we couldn’t find any significant changes.
>
> She was driven mad by the ‘burning sensation’ in her body and the rash. She started looking into it herself (without telling us), and soon read that the antidepressants could be the cause of her symptoms. She took this to her GP. That’s where all the problems began! The GP wanted to taper off the antidepressants without a moment’s hesitation.
>
> This period in a nutshell:
> First suicide attempt - May 2025:
> In June 2024, following a consultation with her GP, my mother began tapering off her sertraline (25mg) after 16 years of use (25mg > 15mg > 10mg > 5mg).
> On 2 January 2025, her daily dose was 5mg, and that’s when things went completely wrong.
> Tremors, a burning sensation in her limbs, panic attacks, agitation, insomnia and confusion.
> Her GP (in consultation with a psychiatrist) advised her to increase the dose from 5mg to 10mg within one day, to 12.5mg two days later and to 25mg four days after that.
> The symptoms got worse and worse, and she was prescribed 15mg of Zyprexa to help her sleep. The next day, 7.5mg of Zyprexa, and then she stopped taking it.
> The sleep helped keep the complications manageable, but they did not disappear.
> Two months later, things went wrong again due to stressful situations in her life, and the GP advised increasing the dose to 50mg of sertraline.
> The symptoms worsened again, so the GP advised increasing the dose to 100mg of sertraline.
> She said she felt as though she was poisoning herself.
> Eventually, on 3 May 2025, she attempted suicide by taking an overdose of sertraline and zyprexa (800mg sertraline + 2 strips of 15mg zyprexa).
>
> She survived and was admitted to an open ward at GGZ Zuyderland Hospital Sittard-Geleen under the care of psychiatrists H.J.H. Bremer and J.M.C. Martens.
> Here, things went from bad to worse.
> They stopped the sertraline and switched to escitalopram. The escitalopram dose was gradually increased daily, starting with 1mg and eventually reaching 10mg.
> After 2 to 3 weeks with no improvement, the dose was increased to 15mg of escitalopram in a single day; this had a devastating effect on my mother.
> We pointed out that the dose increase was only making her worse, and she was quickly reduced back to 10mg.
> A day later, Mr Bremer said they were going to taper her off and try a different medication. Back down to 5mg in a single day.
> Then, later that same day, Mr Martens came in and said: we need to keep trying at 10mg for a while, so back up again. Two different psychiatrists in a single day, saying completely opposite things. The symptoms only got worse.
>
> Second suicide attempt -
> On Monday 9 June 2025, she attempted suicide again, this time by drinking drain cleaner. She fought for her life in the ICU for two months. Seeing your mother like that, because of ‘professionals’, is true hell. Knowing that the same people would have to help us climb out of this even deeper hole. Professionals who pretend they’re doing their best and have your best interests at heart, but lie straight to your face and just want to get off the hook.
>
> Following her admission to the ICU, Mum has made a physical recovery, but she has been left with very severe chemical burns to her oesophagus. She was subsequently transferred to Maastricht UMC for a second opinion and admission to the psychiatric ward, where she is being treated by Professor Marcelis. Compared to previous experiences, this period was a breath of fresh air (a thousand thanks to the great Mrs Mercelis for this!!!). People listened, there was room for discussion, and well-considered decisions were made (Within the centralized framework).
>
> In the first few weeks, a conscious decision was made (at our family’s strong insistence) not to administer any antidepressant medication, with the aim of allowing her nervous system to settle down and so the new practitioners in Maastricht UMC could get to know my mother better. They investigated whether underlying factors were at play, such as trauma or issues from her past. At the same time, she continued to suffer from symptoms such as a burning sensation in her body and head, tremors, panic, confusion and severe insomnia. She also developed anhedonia, stating that she no longer experienced any emotions and wondering whether she would feel anything at all regarding events involving her loved ones. She was aware that this was not reality, but stemmed from her situation. After a few weeks, it was concluded that there were no underlying psychiatric issues and that the symptoms had likely arisen during the tapering off of medication.
>
> Eventually, after 4 weeks, treatment was started with nortriptyline, which was gradually increased to 100 mg, with a therapeutic level of around 70. She was temporarily prescribed lorazepam to help her sleep, but this was discontinued due to its diminishing effectiveness and the risk of dependency. Quetiapine (50 mg) was then introduced, which she is still taking alongside the nortriptyline.
>
> Meanwhile, the severe oesophageal injury played a major role. My mother was unable to eat or drink and was dependent on tube feeding. Symptoms such as extreme vomiting, foul tastes and excessive mucus production made the situation extremely difficult. The oesophagus was completely blocked, it filled up her lungs, and so all fluids had to come back out through the mouth. This naturally also had a major impact on her mental state. These images are etched into our memories.
>
> After four months at the open ward at Maastricht UMC, it was decided in consultation that further mental recovery would largely depend on her physical condition. At that point, she was, however, considerably more stable mentally than before her admission to Maastricht UMC. Her mental are was then, with some reluctance on our part, transferred back to Zuyderland Hospital, not to start all over again, but to provide support. The further psychiatric care she would need is, in fact, region-specific and we could not choose who her practitioners were, we tried to push hard for this.
>
> Due to her oesophageal problems, we were referred to Erasmus MC in Rotterdam. There, it was determined that her oesophagus was severely damaged and narrowed, and that only a complete reconstruction was still possible. In January 2026, she underwent this major operation, during which a section of her large intestine was used to create a new oesophagus. The old oesophagus could not be removed because it was too firmly adhered to the surrounding tissue, and the stomach was too severely damaged to continue using it. The operation was technically successful and she is currently recovering; she is now able to eat and drink small amounts, but still has a long way to go.
>
> However, since the operation, we have again observed a deterioration in her mental state. Shortly after the operation at Erasmus MC, her Nortriptyline levels suddenly dropped sharply, presumably due to a change in absorption due to the hospital admission and all different the pain medication she was given during this hospital stay. In the weeks that followed, this improved somewhat, until a problem with the feeding tube meant she was unable to take her medication for a few days. Almost immediately afterwards, her condition deteriorated again, including thoughts of death and despair. With our intensive support, we helped her through this.
>
> It has now been two months and her condition has stabilised again. However, insomnia remains a major problem. Zuyderland Hospital has advised us to use a low dose of benzodiazepine on an occasional basis. Although we have serious reservations about this, the severity of the insomnia sometimes leaves us with no choice but to accept it. It helps a little from time to time, but does not offer a long-term solution.
>
> **\# CURRENT ISSUES**
> The core problem remains that my mother has been experiencing symptoms all this time that closely resemble prolonged withdrawal symptoms: a burning sensation in the body, electrical sensations in the head, problems with temperature regulation, severe insomnia, constant nervousness and an elevated heart rate of around 100 (whereas previously, throughout her life, it had been around 60). Yet, once again, practitioners seem to be focusing primarily on a depressive disorder, whilst we, just as we did at the time at Maastricht UMC, suspect iatrogenic damage.
>
> Zuyderland Hospital is now once again proposing to start various courses of medication. This causes me great concern, as it feels to us like a repeat of previous treatment pathways in which there was a great deal of experimentation with medication, each time resulting in a deterioration in her condition. My conviction is that her nervous system needs rest above all else after this exceptionally difficult period. We are therefore primarily seeking solutions for her insomnia and, in my assessment, protracted withdrawal symptoms. We have noticed that it is the insomnia in particular that is undermining her the most; a bad night’s sleep significantly exacerbates her symptoms during the day.
>
> She still has to go to Erasmus MC in Rotterdam every week for weekly dilation of her new oesophagus. However, she says she is starting to experience all the symptoms more and more again – from her oesophagus, her intestines and her nervous system. Summer is approaching again, and it is starting to get warmer. She is terrified of this. We continue to emphasise that she must get outside as much as possible, into the sun, practise grounding, keep the lights off in the evenings, get up at sunrise, turn the lights off at sunset, and wear blue-light-blocking glasses. All of this seems to help only minimally, leaving us increasingly unsure of what we can do.
>
> Her current psychiatrist at Zuyderland Hospital continues, to our repeated disapproval, to prescribe her various heavy-duty medications, such as: increasing the Nortriptyline dose from 70 to 100, increasing Quetiapine to 150–300mg, or trying Olanzapine again, starting lithium (20ml > 50ml/100ml), and repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation at Maastricht UMC+ (outpatient treatment of around 30 sessions, often 5 days a week).
>
> We no longer trust these quacks, and we’re afraid that any change to her medication will throw Mum off balance again. We’ve finally got her to a somewhat ‘stable’ state, where her symptoms are just about bearable, and she sleeps for between two and four hours a night.
>
> I want to emphasise that, despite everything, my mother is an exceptionally strong woman. Every day, she tries to stay active by going for walks, taking on small tasks and keeping herself occupied wherever possible. She is finally smiling at us again. Given the circumstances, that is almost a miracle. She is keen to recover and move forward, and she herself keeps saying that she no longer feels depressed, but is exhausted by the symptoms, the disruption to her nervous system and everything that has happened.
>
> I believe that the inconsistency in treatment, rapid increases and decreases in dosage, and daily fluctuations in dosage have led to kindling and hypersensitivity.
> I think that when withdrawal symptoms appeared in January 2025, the nervous system became dysregulated, and all the medication added later disrupted the nervous system even further.
> Trust in the treating professionals has been completely undermined.
> The psychiatrists claim that the previous suicidal thoughts indicate that the original depression from 16 years ago is returning. However, I consider this utter nonsense, as she had never had suicidal thoughts before.
> The suicidal thoughts can be directly traced back to changes in medication, just like all the other symptoms.
>
> So now we have exhausted all treatment options within the centralised healthcare system in the Netherlands. We are exploring every possibility, from psychedelics to cannabinoids, magnetism and electrical therapy. But we are merely laypeople, trying to research what we can, yet it is still beyond our capabilities. We are at a loss as to who or what can help Mum get better. We are so terribly afraid that any intervention we undertake will have a negative impact on her again, and that we will lose control once more, with all the consequences that entails.
>
> **\# URGENT APPEAL TO ANYONE WHO CAN HELP**
> My younger brother (28) and I (31) came across Dr Jack Kruse’s work six months ago. We had been aware of it for some time, but the podcast with Danny Jones and Dr Kruse showed us exactly how the system works, not only in America, but in the Netherlands too. This has given us such enormous strength to fight back against the practitioners and the hospitals. These people may call themselves professionals, but a son who sees his mother being poisoned, who sees his mother attempt suicide twice, is capable of anything. Dr Kruse calls on his Savages to fight. In the small community where we live, my brother and I are fighting against the big system, and they will never win!
> We have completely turned our lives upside down. We are trying to get Mum involved in this too, but because she is so mentally unstable, many things are difficult for her to understand.
>
> Until now, I have been a silent participant on this forum. I have tried to gather as much information as possible, specifically regarding our case. But Mum’s condition is rapidly deteriorating again, and I cannot learn the complex subject matter quickly enough to be able to help her. Hence my cry for help to anyone who can offer us some guidance.
>
> We are open to any suggestions. Whatever it takes to help Mum lead a stable life. We are prepared to travel the world if necessary. But we have no certainty. We can take action, but what if it has negative consequences?
> I am an uninformed person who would very much like to learn. Yes, we have made many mistakes recently, and perhaps you will cringe when reading this message. But we have fought with everything we have, begging the practitioners to consider non-medicinal methods to help her. We have done everything we can to get her out of the centralised system, but the practitioners have completely ignored this.
>
> If anyone is willing to take the time to read this long message in their spare time, you’re my hero! I know this won’t be a miracle cure, but if anyone has any information for us on what we could do to help my mum get better, please enlighten us!!
>
>
> If there are any additional questions to help with a better analysis, please feel free to ask, and I hope I can answer all questions correctly.
>
> Thank you all!
> Sander & Marco, sons of our dear mother Jacqueline

### Sander9992 — 2026-05-11T17:39:21+0300
